q: Organic compounds hold the key to life on the Earth and make up the majority of our bodies.
Macromolecules are formed by smaller molecules connecting together as if they were holding hands.
Simple organic compounds that contain only the elements carbon and hydrogen are called aromatic hydrocarbons.
Alcohols, ethers, aldehydes, ketones, carboxylic acids, and esters are examples of organic compounds where carbon atoms are covalently linked to oxygen atoms.
The characteristic odour of cherries is due to benzaldehyde.
An ether is an organic compound with an $O$ atom bonded to two alkyl groups.
In a symmetrical ether, the two alkyl groups are different ($R$ and $R'$).
Ethers have the general formula $C_{n}H_{2n+2}O$.
In the IUPAC system for ethers, the oxygen atom and the smaller alkyl group are named as an alkoxy substituent.
Ethers can be obtained from alcohol by the elimination of a molecule of water from two molecules of alcohol.
The dehydration of ethanol with a limited amount of sulphuric acid at $140\ ^{\circ}C$ forms ethoxyethane.
Methoxymethane and methoxyethane are colourless liquids at room temperature.
There is no intermolecular hydrogen bonding between ether molecules, making their boiling points much lower than alcohols of similar molecular mass.
The solubility of ethers in water increases as the number of carbon atoms increases.
On heating with concentrated hydrogen iodide ($HI$), the $C-O$ bond in ethers breaks forming alcohol and alkyl iodide.
Ethoxyethane was used for many years as a surgical anaesthetic agent but has now been replaced by safer nonflammable alternatives.
Aldehydes and ketones are organic compounds which contain the carbonyl group, $>C=O$.
In aldehydes, a carbonyl group located at the end of a carbon chain is bonded to at least one hydrogen atom.
In ketones, a carbonyl group is always located at the end of a carbon chain.
Aldehydes and ketones have the general formula $C_{n}H_{2n}O$ and form a homologous series.
The common names of aldehydes are given based on the names of corresponding carboxylic acids.
An alpha ($\alpha$) carbon is a carbon atom bonded to a functional group.
The systematic (IUPAC) names for aldehydes are derived by adding the suffix -al instead of -e to the characteristic stem.
The simplest ketone is called propanone or acetone.
Primary alcohols give ketones when heated with an oxidising agent such as acidified potassium dichromate solution.
The aldehyde product is obtained by distillation to remove it immediately from the reaction mixture to prevent further oxidation to a carboxylic acid.
To produce a ketone, secondary alcohol must be heated with an oxidising agent.
The addition of water to ethyne in the presence of $H_{2}SO_{4}$ and $HgSO_{4}$ produces ethanol.
At room temperature, methanal is a gas and ethanal is a volatile liquid.
Aldehydes are easily oxidised to carboxylic acids with common oxidising agents such as potassium permanganate.
Ketones are easily oxidised under mild conditions using potassium dichromate.
Aldehydes and ketones are easily reduced to corresponding primary and secondary alcohols using lithium aluminium hydride, $LiAlH_{4}$.
$LiAlH_{4}$ is used in dry ether because it reacts violently with water.
Hydrogen cyanide, $HCN$, adds across the carbon-oxygen double bond in aldehydes and ketones to produce hydroxynitriles.
Formalin is a $40\%$ aqueous solution of methanal used as a disinfectant and preservative for biological specimens.
Carboxylic acids are organic compounds with a carboxyl functional group $-COOH$.
Carboxylic acids form a homologous series with the general formula $C_{n}H_{2n}O_{2}$.
Since a carboxylic acid group must always be found at the end of a carbon chain, it is always given the "1" location position in numbering.
Ethanoic acid can be formed by the oxidation of ethanol using atmospheric oxygen in the presence of bacteria (Acetobacter).
Carboxylic acids with up to four carbons are insoluble in water.
Carboxylic acids have high boiling points due to strong intermolecular hydrogen bonds.
Carboxylic acids are weak acids and can react with reactive metals such as sodium to give salt and hydrogen.
Carboxylic acids react with carbonates to give salt, water, and carbon dioxide.
Esters are carboxylic acid derivatives in which the hydroxyl ($-OH$) group has been replaced by an alkoxy ($-OR$) group.
Generally, carboxylic acid can react with alcohol to form ester in a process known as esterification.
The boiling points of esters are higher than those of the corresponding parent acids.
Basic hydrolysis of an ester is a reversible reaction.
Amines are classified as primary, secondary, and tertiary depending on how many alkyl groups are attached to the nitrogen.
Primary and secondary amines have lower boiling points than those of alcohols which have the same number of carbon atoms.
Amides are carboxylic acid derivatives in which the $-OH$ of the carboxylic acid has been replaced by $-NH_{2}, -NHR$, or $-NR_{2}$ of an amine.
The identification of organic compounds is based on simple chemical tests and spectroscopic techniques.
Alkanes show decolourisation when mixed with $1\%$ $KMnO_{4}$ solution.
Alkenes decolourise $1\%$ $KMnO_{4}$ solution.
Alkynes form a red coloured precipitate when passed through ammoniacal $Cu_{2}Cl_{2}$ solution.
Ethers form a yellow coloured precipitate when boiled with $HI$ and then reacted with $AgNO_{3}$.
Aldehydes form a reddish-brown coloured precipitate when heated gently with Fehling's solution.
Ketones give a reddish-brown precipitate when heated gently with Fehling's solution.
The presence of a carbonyl group such as aldehyde or ketone can be easily tested using 2,4-DNP.
If an aldehyde or ketone is present with 2,4-DNP, a deep-orange precipitate is formed.
Fehling's solution is an alkaline solution containing copper(II) ions.
Infrared (IR) spectroscopy is the study of the absorption of light by a compound in the ultraviolet region.
Electromagnetic radiation is characterised by either its frequency or its wavelength.
Wavenumber (measured in $cm^{-1}$) is the reciprocal of the wavelength.
High frequencies, large wavenumbers, and short wavelengths are associated with high energies.
Each spectroscopic technique employs a different kind of electromagnetic radiation.
IR spectroscopy detects frequencies of infrared radiation that are absorbed by a molecule to cause bond vibrations.
Stretching is a vibration that occurs between two bonds and changes the bond angle.
Only stretching and bending motions that change the dipole moment of a molecule give rise to the absorption of infrared radiation.
Homonuclear diatomic molecules like $N_{2}$ are IR active.
Heteronuclear diatomic molecules like $CO$ are IR active because they have dipole moments.
Light atoms vibrate at higher frequencies than heavier atoms.
Multiple bonds vibrate at higher frequencies than single bonds because it takes more energy to stretch a multiple bond.
The instrument used to obtain an infrared spectrum is called an IR spectrometer.
An IR spectrum is a plot of the percent transmittance or absorbance of radiation versus the wavenumber.
The higher bond polarity and more number of bonds in molecules will give a more intense absorption band.
A macromolecule is a very large molecule that contains thousands of atoms or more.
Carbohydrates, proteins, and nucleic acids are natural polymers.
Plastics, fibres, and elastomers are synthetic macromolecules manufactured in industry.
A polymer consists of only one kind of monomers is called a copolymer.
The process of joining together a large number of monomers to form a polymer is called polymerisation.
Addition polymerisation occurs when unsaturated monomers join together without losing any molecules or atoms.
Condensation polymerisation occurs with the removal of a small molecule such as water.
The simplest addition polymer is polyethene produced by the addition polymerisation of ethene monomers.
In polyethene, the carbon-carbon double bonds of the ethene molecules break to form single bonds with other monomers.
Polyvinyl chloride (PVC) is produced from monomers of chloroethene.
Addition polymers are generally soluble in water at low temperatures.
Teflon is heat-resistant and has non-stick properties.
Polystyrene is used to make disposable containers.
Condensation polymerisation is characterised by monomers that contain two different functional groups.
Terylene is an example of a polyester because it contains many ester linkages.
Nylon is the first synthetic fibre made from a dicarboxylic acid and a diamine.
Nylon is a polyamide because it contains many amide linkages.
Proteins are made from thousands of amino acid monomers.
The bond formed between two amino acids is called a peptide bond (amide link).
Condensation polymers often have lower molecular masses than addition polymers.
Plastics are biodegradable and can be easily broken down by bacteria in the soil.
PVC produces corrosive hydrogen chloride gas during combustion.
Biodegradable plastics contain small amounts of starch granules to help them decompose naturally.
Photodegradable plastics incorporate carbonyl groups ($C=O$) that absorb energy from ultraviolet rays to break down.
The number 1 recycling symbol stands for polyethene terephthalate (PETE/PET).
